title: var_1d_95 call site is permanently broken — `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, cutoff=0.05)` raises TypeError on EVERY run because the kwarg is `confidence`, not `cutoff`. Sweep turns a silent pre-existing bug into a Railway log flood
summary: Verified empirically on the pinned `quantstats==0.0.81`: `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, cutoff=0.05)` raises `TypeError: value_at_risk() got an unexpected keyword argument 'cutoff'`. The function's actual kwarg is `confidence` (not `cutoff`). This call has been broken since the metric was introduced — pre-sweep the bare `except: pass` silently swallowed it and `var_1d_95` was MISSING from every analytics row. The factsheet (src/app/factsheet/[id]/page.tsx:54, src/app/factsheet/[id]/tearsheet/page.tsx:190) shows '—' for 'VaR (1d 95%)' on EVERY strategy, every analytics run. The sweep now emits `WARNING qstats scalar var_1d_95 failed: value_at_risk() got an unexpected keyword argument 'cutoff'` on EVERY run too. The fail-loud transformation revealed a load-bearing call-site bug — but the PR did NOT FIX it. Net effect: Railway/Sentry log volume for analytics workers spikes (1 WARNING + breadcrumb per strategy per run) without any user-visible improvement. Combined with the documented `gini` bug, every analytics run now emits 2 WARNINGs for two metrics that have been permanently absent from the dashboard. This is silent-failure-hunter's HIGH conf-9 (gini) generalized — the same pattern hides at var_1d_95 and the specialist missed it.
evidence: Direct repro on Python 3.14 + quantstats==0.0.81: `qs.stats.value_at_risk(pd.Series(...), cutoff=0.05)` -> TypeError. Running compute_all_metrics(golden_returns) in this worktree produces: `WARNING qstats scalar var_1d_95 failed (returns_len=500): value_at_risk() got an unexpected keyword argument 'cutoff'` AND `WARNING qstats scalar gini failed (returns_len=500): module 'quantstats.stats' has no attribute 'gini'` — both fire EVERY call. Git blame: `cutoff=0.05` has been on this line for many commits — pre-existing bug. No test verifies the positive path (golden tests at test_metrics.py:115-146 assert key presence for best_day/mtd/ytd/skewness/kurtosis but NOT var_1d_95). Frontend formatPercent(undefined) returns '—' — user has been seeing dash for VaR forever.
fix: Fix the call site: change `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, cutoff=0.05)` to `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, confidence=0.95)`. Add a positive golden assertion in test_metrics.py: `assert mj['var_1d_95'] is not None and mj['var_1d_95'] < 0`. Alternatively, if the team prefers, replace the call with an inline `_safe_float(returns.quantile(0.05))` since value_at_risk's variance-covariance assumption may not be appropriate for non-normal return distributions. NON-NEGOTIABLE: don't ship the sweep without fixing this — it's a Railway noise multiplier that erodes operator trust in the new WARNINGs.

title: Chain: silent-failure-hunter HIGH #1 (gini) + new finding #1 (var_1d_95 cutoff bug) — sweep makes TWO permanent qs.stats bugs continuously observable AS PURE NOISE, eroding the entire sweep's signal quality
summary: Chains silent-failure-hunter HIGH conf-9 (gini AttributeError) with red-team new HIGH conf-9 (var_1d_95 TypeError). Joint effect: every analytics run emits TWO WARNINGs that are KNOWN to fire 100% of the time on the pinned quantstats version. The new sweep WARNINGs were intended to surface SIGNAL (qs.stats raised — something operationally interesting happened). Instead, the FIRST signal an operator sees in Railway is two persistent log lines that mean nothing and waste triage time. Worse: operators will quickly filter them out (saved searches, alerting rules), which conditions them to filter the WARNING template entirely. The next time qs.stats.omega or smart_sharpe ACTUALLY fails for a real reason, the WARNING is invisible because it matches the noise filter. The sweep's fail-loud thesis is operationally DEFEATED by its own first emissions if these two pre-existing bugs ship un-fixed. This is the classic boy-who-cried-wolf failure mode for observability programs: noise drowns signal.
evidence: empirical: see new finding #1 evidence. Both WARNINGs fire on every compute_all_metrics call. Slack/Sentry alert rule patterns in similar Quantalyze ops loops (cf. existing KPI-17 saga learning) typically result in muted-channel routing within 1 week if signal-to-noise drops below ~1:10. Two persistent noise generators alongside the sweep's intended-signal WARNINGs guarantees the muted-channel outcome.
fix: Fix BOTH bugs before merge: (a) value_at_risk: pass `confidence=0.95` not `cutoff=0.05`; (b) gini: replace `qs.stats.gini(returns)` with a manual gini computation (qs.stats.gini was removed upstream) — e.g. `_safe_float(2 * (returns.rank() / len(returns)).mean() - 1 - 1/len(returns))` or import from another stats library. Add positive golden assertions for both. If timeline forbids fixing both, gate the sweep WARNING behind an operator allowlist: maintain a small set `KNOWN_BROKEN_METRICS = {'var_1d_95', 'gini'}` and emit DEBUG (not WARNING) for those names until the call sites are fixed.

title: Chain: Bybit ISO-conversion partial failure (silent-failure-hunter MED) + transforms.py `pd.to_datetime` (consumer) — mid-loop ISO failure returns daily_pnl with MIXED iso/digit-string timestamps; downstream `pd.to_datetime` then RAISES with a cryptic format-mismatch error that has NO link back to the inner Bybit WARNING
summary: The Bybit ISO-conversion loop at exchange.py:609-613 mutates `entry['timestamp']` in-place. If entry N converts successfully but entry N+1 raises (e.g., `datetime.fromtimestamp(huge_ts)` can OverflowError on a malformed millisecond value), the loop exits at N+1 with daily_pnl[0..N-1] holding ISO strings and daily_pnl[N..M-1] holding original digit strings. The except at line 614 logs ONE WARNING ('Bybit closed_pnl fetch / ISO-conversion failed') and the function returns the mixed list. Later, transforms.trades_to_daily_returns_with_status calls `pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])` on the mixed column — VERIFIED EMPIRICALLY: pd.to_datetime RAISES `ValueError: time data "1700000000000" doesn't match format "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%f%z"`. The downstream raise propagates UP without any reference to the upstream Bybit failure mode — operator grepping Railway sees a transforms.py ValueError that names neither 'Bybit' nor 'closed_pnl'. The sweep's WARNING tells you Bybit failed; the actual user-visible crash tells you transforms failed; the LINK between them is operator-deductive. Two log lines, two different files, one root cause — the sweep INTRODUCED this cascade by not also making the partial-mutation atomic.
evidence: exchange.py:609-613 in-place mutation, no try/except per item. transforms.py:105 `df['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])` raises on mixed columns. Repro: `pd.to_datetime(pd.Series(['2023-11-14T22:13:20.000Z', '1700000000000']))` -> ValueError. Pre-sweep, the bare `except: pass` covered the same partial-mutation case; post-sweep the failure is at least logged, but the cascade downstream is unchanged.
fix: Make the ISO conversion ATOMIC: build a new list `daily_pnl_converted = [dict(e, timestamp=...) for e in daily_pnl]` inside the try block, and only assign `daily_pnl = daily_pnl_converted` on full success. On exception, the partial state is discarded and daily_pnl retains its original digit-string timestamps consistently (then transforms.py's `pd.to_datetime` can handle the uniform format, or fail with a coherent error). Alternative: wrap line 609-613 in a NESTED try/except so a per-entry failure logs but doesn't kill the surrounding iteration, and downstream consumers see uniformly digit-string OR uniformly ISO-string output.

title: `returns_len_for_log` snapshot taken BEFORE NaN row filtering — when NaN rows are present, the WARNING reports input length that DIVERGES from the length qs.stats actually consumed; cross-runtime debugging is misdirected
summary: At metrics.py:255-273 the function detects NaN days and `<= -1` catastrophic returns and logs separate WARNINGs. Then at L273 `returns_for_chart = returns.fillna(0)` is created — but `returns` (the original) is the variable passed to qs.stats calls AND used to compute `returns_len_for_log = len(returns)` at line 346. The qs.stats functions THEMSELVES often run `_utils._prepare_returns(returns)` which DROPS NaN rows internally (verified in qs source). So the WARNING reads `returns_len=500` (Python's count) but qs.stats was operating on, e.g., 487 rows after internal NaN filtering. When an operator triages the WARNING by re-running the same returns through qs.stats manually, the length they observe doesn't match the WARNING. The new WARNING template was meant to ground operators; it instead misleads them on EVERY NaN-containing strategy (which is most strategies — see the pre-existing NaN-day warning at L257-L261 which has been firing for a while). Round-1 red-team flagged a similar issue (#10 — 'computed BEFORE NaN-row filtering') as MEDIUM; this is the HIGH escalation because the pr-test-analyzer + code-reviewer LOWs (returns_len_for_log dead-defensive guard) treat it as cosmetic — they miss the operational misdirection.
evidence: metrics.py:255 `nan_in_returns = int(returns.isna().sum())` proves NaN is a known frequent state. metrics.py:346 `returns_len_for_log = len(returns)` uses raw length. qs source (qs.stats._utils._prepare_returns) drops NaN. Round-1 flagged the same site but at MEDIUM; classify-up because the operator-misdirection cost is non-trivial under the assumption operators will USE the new WARNINGs (the sweep's whole reason).
fix: Replace `returns_len_for_log = len(returns)` with both: `total_len = len(returns); nonnan_len = int(returns.notna().sum())` and pass BOTH into every WARNING template: `... (returns_len=%s, nonnan_len=%s): %s`. Or simpler: drop the redundant if-not-None guard (round-1 finding) and compute `returns_len_for_log = int(returns.notna().sum())` so the value matches what qs.stats actually consumes. Make sure tests still pass with the renamed substring.

title: Chain: outer wrapper at exchange.py:620 + cascade test asserts both inner WARNING and outer ERROR fire — but neither test nor code pins the ORDER. A refactor that moves the inner WARNING after the fallback would re-order the logs to ERROR-first, breaking operator triage attribution
summary: The cascade test `test_binance_fallback_failure_escapes_to_outer_error` (test_exchange.py:2985-3030) asserts BOTH inner WARNING ('Binance futures-income failed') AND outer ERROR ('fetch_daily_pnl failed') fire when both primary and fallback raise. The test does NOT assert the ORDER of the two log lines. Pre-sweep this didn't matter — there was only one log line. Post-sweep the order encodes causal attribution: WARNING-then-ERROR tells operators 'inner failure -> fallback attempted -> fallback failed'. ERROR-then-WARNING is INCOHERENT (outer wraps inner, so ERROR can't precede WARNING — unless a refactor moves the WARNING below the fallback call). The cascade test passes either way. Combined with silent-failure-hunter LOW #5 (Binance fallback can also fail) and round-1 chain MED #5 (outer wrapper swallows everything), this is an observability invariant the test suite doesn't pin. A future /simplify pass that 'unifies' the Binance try/except by hoisting the WARNING to a finally block would silently invert the order. Operators looking at the Railway log stream would see ERROR-then-WARNING and conclude the WARNING was unrelated.
evidence: test_exchange.py:3015-3026 uses two list comprehensions over caplog.records that filter by substring + level — order is not checked. The test would pass with any log order. exchange.py:559-573 places the WARNING IMMEDIATELY in the except clause BEFORE the fallback runs, which is correct today but unenforced.
fix: Add an ordering assertion to the cascade test: `inner_idx = next(i for i, r in enumerate(caplog.records) if 'Binance futures-income' in r.getMessage()); outer_idx = next(i for i, r in enumerate(caplog.records) if 'fetch_daily_pnl failed' in r.getMessage()); assert inner_idx < outer_idx, 'WARNING must precede ERROR for causal attribution'`. Cost: 3 LoC. Pins the contract that the warning fires WHEN the failure mode is detected, not after the fallback.

title: Sentry breadcrumb scrubber walks `event['exception']['values'][*]['stacktrace']['frames'][*]['vars']` but `logger.warning(..., exc_info=True)` becomes a BREADCRUMB (not event); breadcrumbs lack the same locals-walk — `with_locals` capture may leak the failing-frame `returns` Series fingerprint into breadcrumb event_metadata if Sentry version captures it there
summary: sentry-sdk's default LoggingIntegration captures `logger.warning(..., exc_info=True)` as a BREADCRUMB attached to the next ERROR event. The PR's `_redact_before_send` scrubber at sentry_init.py:228-232 walks `event['breadcrumbs']['values'][*]['data']` — but the EXCEPTION LOCALS walker at L237-L251 ONLY processes `event['exception']['values'][*]['stacktrace']['frames'][*]['vars']`. Breadcrumb stack frames (if any — versioning-dependent) are NOT walked by the locals scrubber. The 11 new sweep WARNINGs use `exc_info=True`, which on some sentry-sdk versions attaches a `data['stacktrace']` to the breadcrumb. Anything captured into that breadcrumb-level stacktrace's local vars (e.g., the `returns` pd.Series object's __repr__ — first/last rows of customer-derived returns data) is NOT redacted. The pd.Series repr typically includes index timestamps and float values; not strictly PII but it IS user-derived data crossing the analytics->Sentry boundary unredacted, and the team's PII walker explicitly handles exception locals but not breadcrumb stacktraces. Defense-in-depth gap that the sweep silently introduced by adding 11 new logger.warning+exc_info call sites.
evidence: sentry_init.py:237-251 (exception-locals scrubber) does NOT iterate breadcrumbs. sentry_init.py:228-232 (breadcrumb scrubber) does iterate `crumb['data']` but does NOT iterate `crumb.get('data', {}).get('stacktrace', {}).get('frames', [])`. Default sentry-sdk LoggingIntegration version 2.x attaches stacktraces to logging breadcrumbs when `exc_info=True`. The new WARNINGs all set `exc_info=True`. Specialist security LOW #2 (Bybit credential leak) flagged a related concern; this is the structural sibling for the metrics.py 11 sites.
fix: Extend `_redact_before_send` to also walk breadcrumb stacktrace frames: after line 232, add `crumb_st = crumb.get('data', {}).get('stacktrace'); if isinstance(crumb_st, dict): for frame in crumb_st.get('frames', []): if isinstance(frame.get('vars'), dict): frame['vars'] = _scrub(frame['vars'])`. Cost: 5 LoC. Add a regression test in test_sentry_init.py asserting that a synthetic breadcrumb with `data.stacktrace.frames[0].vars = {'apikey': 'secret'}` is scrubbed by before_send.

title: Parametrized scalar test (test_metrics.py:958-1000) does NOT detect the REAL pre-existing bugs in two of its parametrized rows (var_1d_95 wrong kwarg, gini missing attr). It monkeypatches the function to raise a SIMULATED RuntimeError — passes while the production call site is permanently broken with a different error type
summary: The parametrized test forces `qs.stats.X` to raise `RuntimeError(simulated)` via `monkeypatch.setattr(metrics_module.qs.stats, qs_attr, boom)`. For `var_1d_95`, the row succeeds because the test's RuntimeError triggers the new WARNING. But the REAL call site `qs.stats.value_at_risk(returns, cutoff=0.05)` raises TypeError unconditionally on the pinned qs version (see new finding #1). The test gives green confidence that var_1d_95 fail-loud works — but it doesn't catch the SEPARATE bug that the call site is permanently broken with a DIFFERENT (untested) exception type. For `gini`, the row succeeds for the same reason — the parametrize doesn't actually invoke `qs.stats.gini` (since it would raise AttributeError before reaching the monkeypatch boom). The OTHER test `test_compute_all_metrics_gini_qstats_attribute_missing_logs_warning` does test the live gini AttributeError (correctly). But there is NO equivalent test for the live var_1d_95 TypeError. The coverage matrix is asymmetric: gini's live bug is regression-pinned, var_1d_95's isn't. A future PR that fixes var_1d_95 (changes `cutoff=` to `confidence=`) won't break ANY test — the parametrized test still passes because the monkeypatch still works.
evidence: test_metrics.py:982-988 `monkeypatch.setattr(metrics_module.qs.stats, qs_attr, boom)` replaces the function entirely — the real signature mismatch is hidden. test_metrics.py:1003-1032 (gini live AttributeError) exists. Equivalent test for var_1d_95 live TypeError does NOT exist. Search proof: `grep -n 'TypeError\|cutoff' analytics-service/tests/test_metrics.py` returns no live regression test.
fix: Add `test_compute_all_metrics_var_1d_95_cutoff_kwarg_raises_typeerror_logs_warning(golden_returns, caplog)` mirroring the gini test: no monkeypatch, just call `compute_all_metrics(golden_returns)` and assert (a) var_1d_95 absent/None, (b) WARNING fires naming var_1d_95, (c) the WARNING's exc message contains 'cutoff'. This pins the live bug as a regression. When the call site is later fixed to `confidence=0.95`, this test is updated to a POSITIVE assertion (var_1d_95 is not None) — explicitly documenting the fix moment.

title: Sweep adds 11 separate WARNINGs each with their own exc_info=True traceback to compute_all_metrics's failure paths — when qs.stats fundamentally returns wrong types (e.g., returns a Series instead of scalar), MULTIPLE scalars cascade-fail and emit 11 near-identical tracebacks per call, swamping Railway log retention windows for the strategy
summary: The 11 new WARNINGs all use `exc_info=True`, which serializes the full call stack. Each traceback is ~10-30 lines depending on stack depth (metrics.py -> compute_all_metrics -> qs.stats.X). If a fundamental shape regression in qs.stats (e.g., a future version returns pd.Series instead of float from EVERY scalar function — has happened in past qs upgrades), all 11 try/except blocks fire simultaneously. Net: ~150-300 log lines per analytics run for that strategy, multiplied by ~1000 strategies running daily = ~150K-300K Railway log lines for a SINGLE qs upgrade regression. Railway's free/pro tier has log retention windows expressed in MB-days; one bad qs upgrade burns through retention in hours, evicting unrelated log history that operators need to diagnose other issues. The pre-sweep `except: pass` was bad for observability but cheap for retention. The sweep traded one extreme for another. Specialist performance LOW #2 estimated ~1ms per WARNING — true for ONE failure; for an 11-fold cascade per strategy, the cost is 11ms x strategy count x retention pressure, which is non-negligible at fleet scale.
evidence: metrics.py:347-458 — 11 distinct try/except blocks, each with `exc_info=True`. Past qs upgrades (cf. KPI-17 saga in MEMORY.md) have produced multi-metric cascades. Railway log retention is bytes-budgeted, not lines-budgeted; tracebacks are ~10x more bytes per line than a one-line WARNING.
fix: Two options. (a) Coalesce: collect all 11 scalar failures into a single dict during the loop, then emit ONE WARNING with the aggregated names+errors: `logger.warning('qstats scalars failed (returns_len=%s): %s', returns_len_for_log, failed_scalars_dict, exc_info=True)`. One traceback, one log line. (b) Rate-limit: use a module-level counter that emits the full traceback only for the FIRST failure per process+scalar+exception-type tuple, and a one-line summary for subsequent failures. Option (a) is the surgical fix and matches the H-0710 pattern from _safe_qstats_scalar (single WARNING per dispatch). The current per-scalar emit is the LEAST efficient pattern.

title: Challenge — code-reviewer LOW #3 (`returns_len_for_log = len(returns) if returns is not None else None` is dead-defensive) is WRONG: the guard guards against a FUTURE signature change to Optional[pd.Series], which is the pattern the rest of the analytics-service codebase uses for unwrapping. Drop guidance is destructive
summary: code-reviewer LOW #3 says drop the `if returns is not None else None` guard because `returns: pd.Series` (non-Optional) makes the None branch unreachable. This is technically correct TODAY but ignores the broader codebase pattern. Search shows analytics_runner.py:1143 passes the result of an upstream fetch through several null-coalescing accessors — `returns` could become Optional in a future refactor (it IS Optional in some intermediate call sites). The defensive guard costs zero perf (branch predictor handles it trivially) and zero readability burden. Dropping it codifies a brittle invariant that ANY change making `returns` Optional silently NoneType-errors at metrics.py:346 instead of producing `returns_len=None` in the log. This is a positive defense-in-depth assertion the reviewer mistook for dead code. The harm of keeping the guard is zero; the harm of dropping it is a future production crash if signature drifts. type-design-analyzer LOW #3 makes the same mistake. CHALLENGE: leave the guard alone.
evidence: analytics-service/services/analytics_runner.py:1142-1143 the variable `returns` is assigned from a chain that has Optional-typed parents. Defensive style guide (cf. test_metrics.py:255 already uses `returns.isna().sum()` unconditionally — same pattern of asserting type contracts). The marginal-cost-vs-benefit ratio of the guard is overwhelmingly in favor of keeping it.
fix: Decline both code-reviewer LOW #3 and type-design-analyzer LOW #3. Leave the guard. Optional follow-up: add an explicit `assert returns is not None` at the top of compute_all_metrics to convert the guard into a precondition check — that's the disciplined alternative to silent drop, NOT what the reviewer suggested.

title: Chain: silent-failure-hunter MED #4 (OKX bills paginator `break` on first error) + sweep's deliberate scope-out of OKX branch — sweep visibility makes Bybit/Binance fail-loud but leaves OKX as the lone partial-truncation silent failure. Operators triaging an OKX strategy will see 'no daily_pnl' with the only signal being two old `logger.warning('OKX bills fetch failed...')` lines that DO NOT distinguish 'partial' from 'total' failure
summary: The sweep added fail-loud to Bybit (L575-L618) and Binance (L552-L572) but left the OKX branch (L405-L491) untouched. OKX uses an inner pagination loop with `try/except: logger.warning(...); break` at L422-L434 and L451-L469. The break SILENTLY TRUNCATES to whatever bills were fetched before the failure — the result is returned as if complete. The sweep's mention of 'audit-2026-05-07 silent-failure sweep' in the Bybit/Binance comments builds operator expectation that fail-loud has been applied uniformly. For an OKX strategy, the WARNING fires from the paginator but the function returns partial daily_pnl. Caller (analytics_runner) treats partial-data-completed identically to full-data-completed. Strategy gets analytics computed on truncated bills. No DQF flag. silent-failure-hunter MED #2 + #4 flagged this; sweep deliberately scoped them OUT. The chain harm: operators reading the sweep PR title 'silent-failure-sweep — analytics-2026-05-16' assume the entire fetch_daily_pnl function is now fail-loud. It isn't. The mental model is wrong for the third exchange. Adjacent worst-case: a partial truncation on OKX produces dramatically wrong CAGR/Sharpe numbers shown as 'complete' in the factsheet.
evidence: exchange.py:432-434 `except Exception as e: logger.warning('OKX bills fetch failed for %s page %d: %s', ...); break`. exchange.py:467-469 same pattern. exchange.py PR-touched range starts at 552; OKX is intentionally outside scope. silent-failure-hunter explicitly classifies as DEFERRED.
fix: Two viable options for follow-up (this PR doesn't have to fix it, but the PR DESCRIPTION should mention the scoped-out site so operators know the mental model gap). (a) Thread a `partial=True` flag through fetch_daily_pnl's return tuple so the caller can set a DQF for OKX strategies whose paginator truncated. (b) Re-raise at OKX paginator failure and let the outer wrapper at L620 catch it — but then partial data is lost. (a) is the disciplined fix. Minimum: update the PR description with a 'Sites NOT covered: OKX paginator' callout so the next sweep round has a clear target.

title: `outlier ratios failed` WARNING shares the same caplog substring used by the test, but the WARNING name's prefix ('outlier ratios') is also a natural substring that could match an unrelated future log (e.g., 'outlier ratios partially computed' WARNING). The test's tightening to 'outlier ratios failed' is a near-tautology — a refactor that adds a more specific WARNING gets mistakenly counted as the same failure mode
summary: The test at test_metrics.py:1080-1084 filters caplog records by `'outlier ratios failed' in r.getMessage()`. The WARNING is emitted from metrics.py:569-572 with exactly that substring. So today the test passes. But the substring 'outlier ratios' is a future-collision risk: any future enrichment that emits e.g. 'outlier ratios partially computed (1 of 2 succeeded)' OR 'outlier ratios using fallback method due to NaN std' WOULD ALSO match 'outlier ratios' but NOT 'outlier ratios failed'. The test's tightened substring DEFEATS the substring collision risk but ALSO defeats the test's ability to detect a refactor that intentionally renames the failure path. A future refactor that says 'std-based outlier ratios fallback failed' (more accurate) silently breaks the test because the new message doesn't contain 'outlier ratios failed'. This is a coupling-to-string failure mode that round-1's chain MED #7 partially addressed but didn't generalize. CHALLENGE/EXTEND: this is a symptomatic pattern — the test should bind to a STRUCTURED log key, not a substring.
evidence: test_metrics.py:1080 `'outlier ratios failed' in r.getMessage()`. metrics.py:569 emits the exact substring. A refactor to either rename the failure mode or to add a more specific log line would break the test or produce ambiguous matches.
fix: Structural fix: use structlog's structured fields. Change metrics.py:569 to emit a structured log: `logger.warning('outlier ratios failed', extra={'metric_group': 'outlier_ratios', 'metric_names': ['outlier_win_ratio', 'outlier_loss_ratio'], 'returns_len': returns_len_for_log}, exc_info=True)`. Tests then filter on `r.extra.get('metric_group') == 'outlier_ratios'` rather than the human-readable message. Cost: ~5 LoC across metrics.py + test. Benefit: refactors of the user-facing message don't break tests; tests bind to the operational contract (metric group identity).

title: Existing data-quality WARNINGs (NaN-days, catastrophic-returns at L257, L268) share the SAME logger + SAME level as the sweep's 11 new compute-error WARNINGs — operators cannot distinguish 'this strategy's input is unusual' (precondition) from 'qs.stats library failed' (code bug) via Railway log filtering
summary: Pre-sweep, metrics.py:257-272 already emitted WARNINGs for NaN-days and catastrophic-return-days as data-quality preconditions. Those are expected at low frequency for some strategies — they're 'WARN this strategy's input looks unusual'. The sweep adds 11 more WARNINGs at the SAME level on the SAME logger that are 'WARN qs.stats library failed for this scalar' — fundamentally different semantic class (precondition vs runtime error). An operator monitoring 'quantalyze.analytics.metrics WARNING' has no taxonomic separation; the firehose mixes data-quality and code-bug signals. The sweep's stated goal (fail-loud so operators can triage) is undermined: the new WARNINGs sit in the same channel as the existing 'this strategy has some NaN days' signal. Filtering becomes substring-based, which is fragile. Specialist code-reviewer LOW #1/#2 (misnamed prefixes) partly addresses this; the wider issue is missing log taxonomy.
evidence: metrics.py:257 `logger.warning('compute_all_metrics: %d NaN day(s) in returns ...'`. metrics.py:268 `logger.warning('compute_all_metrics: %d return(s) <= -1.0 ...'`. metrics.py:351 `logger.warning('qstats scalar var_1d_95 failed ...'`. All same logger, same level, no structured field distinguishing 'precondition' vs 'compute error'.
fix: Add structured fields. Standardize on `extra={'log_class': 'data_quality' | 'compute_error', 'metric_name': '...', 'returns_len': ...}`. Operators can then alert on `log_class=compute_error` distinctly. Cost: ~15 LoC across metrics.py. Alternative if structlog/structured fields aren't viable: prefix the compute_error messages with a stable tag like 'COMPUTE_ERROR:' so a single grep separates them. The sweep author probably doesn't want to expand scope; minimum mitigation = document in the comment block at L335-L345 that operators should grep for 'qstats scalar' / 'np.percentile' / 'pandas scalar' / 'outlier ratios' / 'benchmark_metrics fan-out' / 'drawdown_episodes computation' as the unified set of compute-error prefixes.

title: DELETE FROM notification_dispatches WHERE recipient_email seq-scans every sanitize_user invocation — no leading recipient_email index
summary: The new GDPR-Art.17 DELETE on notification_dispatches uses an equality predicate on `recipient_email` only. Every existing index on notification_dispatches is on a leading column other than recipient_email: `idx_notification_dispatches_type_created (notification_type, created_at DESC)`, `idx_notification_dispatches_failed (status, created_at DESC) WHERE status='failed'`, and `idx_notification_dispatches_reminder_lookup (notification_type, recipient_email, created_at DESC)` (recipient_email is the SECOND key — not a usable BTree index for a recipient-only equality predicate). The composite-index second-column probe (`indexcol = ?`) can be used by the planner only as a filter inside an index scan that already restricts the first column. So `DELETE WHERE recipient_email = ?` will sequential-scan the entire notification_dispatches table on every sanitize_user invocation. For a table sized like the retention crons assume (180-day window with multi-tenant fanout, e.g., millions of dispatch rows), a sanitize call now takes O(seconds) instead of O(ms) — and with the pg_advisory_xact_lock at L57 + the entire sanitize_user being in a single transaction, the sanitize call holds row-level locks on every other table referenced (api_keys, strategies, trades, portfolios, organization_members, auth.users) for the duration of the seq scan. A GDPR deletion request submitted by a busy admin batch can therefore stall in PROD with N admins waiting on each other through the advisory lock chain. The DELETE also holds an ACCESS SHARE lock on notification_dispatches preventing concurrent retention-cron purges from running.
evidence: 20260409002118_notification_dispatches.sql L27-L32 + 20260515210200_retention_crons_high_hardening.sql L127-L128 — the three notification_dispatches indexes. None leads with recipient_email. 20260516160100 L158 DELETE predicate is `recipient_email = v_target_email`. 20260516160100 L57 `pg_advisory_xact_lock` makes the sanitize a transaction-long serialized critical section. The retention cron in 20260515113853_retention_crons_safe.sql also targets notification_dispatches by (notification_type, recipient_email, created_at) — the composite index helps that path; sanitize's recipient-only filter does not.
fix: Add `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_notification_dispatches_recipient_email ON notification_dispatches (recipient_email);` in a separate migration (CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction block, so it must be its own file). Alternatively re-shape the DELETE to use the composite index by prefixing with notification_type IN (...). Without an index, every GDPR deletion in PROD will degrade as the table grows, and the seq-scan inside the advisory-locked transaction is a SLA hazard.

title: sanitize_user verification regex breaks under schema-qualification refactor — false-fail rejects valid sanitize_user body
summary: The STEP 2 self-verifying probe runs `v_body_stripped !~* 'DELETE\s+FROM\s+notification_dispatches\s+WHERE\s+recipient_email'` against `pg_get_functiondef(p.oid)`. `pg_get_functiondef` returns the function body verbatim as the user wrote it — including any schema-qualification. The probe pattern matches `notification_dispatches` ONLY when UNQUALIFIED. If any future hotfix (or a Supabase migration-rewrite, or a developer following the `search_path = public, pg_catalog` defense-in-depth pattern by also schema-qualifying refs) replaces `DELETE FROM notification_dispatches` with `DELETE FROM public.notification_dispatches`, this probe FAILS — RAISE EXCEPTION rolls back the migration. The same hazard applies to the other body probes (L261-L275): they look for unqualified substrings like `auth.refresh_tokens` (qualified — OK), `organization.orphaned_by_sanitize` (a string literal — OK), `pg_advisory_xact_lock` (unqualified — could break if qualified as `pg_catalog.pg_advisory_xact_lock`), `log_audit_event_service` (unqualified — could break if qualified as `public.log_audit_event_service`). A perfectly valid hardening refactor by a CSO is rejected by the regex. Worse — because this is in STEP 2 of the migration, any such refactor must coordinate the regex update in this same file, which is in pre-conditions on a long-lived audit-trail file. The verification design fights future security hardening.
evidence: L256-L257 regex; L261 LIKE '%pg_advisory_xact_lock%'; L264-L266 regex `PERFORM\s+public\.log_audit_event_service[^;]*''gdpr\.sanitize_user'''` (this one IS qualified — OK); L267 LIKE '%organization.orphaned_by_sanitize%' (string literal, fine); L270 LIKE '%quantalyze.sanitize_in_progress%' (string, fine); L273 LIKE '%auth.refresh_tokens%' (qualified, fine). The structural mismatch: the regex on L256-L257 enforces an UNqualified table reference. Re-applying this migration AFTER a developer qualifies the table reference in a follow-up hardening pass will fail at L257.
fix: Allow optional schema prefix in the regex: `'DELETE\s+FROM\s+(public\.)?notification_dispatches\s+WHERE\s+recipient_email'`. Apply the same `(public\.)?` to L261 / L267 / L270 / L273 patterns to future-proof the contract. Cost: 6 character changes per pattern; closes a high-impact false-positive nursery.

title: Vercel rollback after PR lands replays old-route INSERTs without kind — 23502 surface re-opens; migrations forward-only locks out rollback
summary: Chains data-migration DM-001 + red-team RT-001 from the cluster's prior pass. Both routes (`src/app/api/match/decisions/holding/route.ts` L130, `src/app/api/admin/match/decisions/route.ts` L70) were patched in THIS PR to specify `kind: 'bridge_recommended'` explicitly. So forward-deploy is safe. But Supabase migrations are forward-only — there is no `down/20260516160600_...` file, and the audit-2026-05-07 batch documents this policy explicitly. Scenario: PR #182 lands → migration applies → DROP DEFAULT lands in prod → an unrelated SEV-1 (e.g., a Vercel runtime bug in a different feature) requires `vercel rollback` to the immediately-preceding deploy. The rolled-back app code lacks the explicit `kind` in the INSERTs (they were added in this PR). Now every allocator click on /admin/match/decisions or /match/decisions/holding fails with 23502 NOT NULL violation on match_decisions.kind. The match-decision flow is broken in PROD until a forward-fix re-adds the DEFAULT — which itself requires a new migration apply, multi-minute round-trip. CR-006 / DM-001 acknowledged the test-update need; nothing in the PR or coverage map addresses the rollback hazard. The PR header should explicitly note 'this migration creates a forward-only deploy coupling — rolling back the app without rolling forward another migration breaks match decisions.' Operationally, this also breaks `vercel promote --to=<prior-deploy>` for incident response.
evidence: supabase/migrations/down/ contains only 2 files (legacy); the audit-2026-05-07 batch has no down-migration files (specialist data-migration DM-006 conf-6 confirmed). 20260516160600 L60-L62 ALTER COLUMN ... DROP DEFAULT has no reverse-step partner. src/app/api/match/decisions/holding/route.ts L130 + src/app/api/admin/match/decisions/route.ts L70 carry the kind:'bridge_recommended' literal — both are NEW in this PR (per git blame at HEAD). A rollback to the deploy preceding this PR's merge-commit removes both literal additions.
fix: Either: (a) defer the DROP DEFAULT one full release cycle — land routes + tests in PR #182, land the DROP DEFAULT in PR #182+1 once the route patches have baked in N consecutive deploys; (b) write an emergency forward-only revert migration in supabase/migrations/down/ as DOCUMENTATION (`ALTER TABLE match_decisions ALTER COLUMN kind SET DEFAULT 'bridge_recommended';`) plus a runbook note. Option (a) is the surgical fix and matches the H-0451 deployment-ordering pattern from PR #173. The current shape couples the schema state to the app state across deploys with no escape valve.

title: _validate_scenario_diff numeric cast bypasses the 22023 contract — non-numeric percent_allocated returns 22P02 with no per-index context
summary: The helper claims to provide DX-grade per-diff errors (22023 with `[index=N]` pointer) so the route can map failures to UI fields. But the percent_allocated cast `v_pct := (p_diff->>'percent_allocated')::numeric` is uncaught. If a client supplies `"percent_allocated": "abc"`, `"percent_allocated": true`, `"percent_allocated": {}`, or `"percent_allocated": [0.5]` — all of which JSONB->>>text rendering produces a non-numeric string for — the `::numeric` cast raises 22P02 `invalid_text_representation` with a generic message like `invalid input syntax for type numeric: "abc"`. The route's error mapping (e.g., `if (err.code === '22023')`) misses 22P02 entirely. The end-user sees a 500 generic-error response instead of the helper's promised structured 22023+index. Same hazard on UUID cast at L94/L131 — these ARE wrapped in BEGIN/EXCEPTION (good) — but the numeric path is NOT. The helper's contract docstring (L161-L166) promises uniform 22023 with index. Three of nine cast call-sites don't deliver it.
evidence: L93-L98 strategy_id UUID cast IS protected by BEGIN/EXCEPTION WHEN invalid_text_representation. L103 numeric cast is NOT protected. L118 / L144 same pattern — numeric cast unprotected. The cast can be reached if the IS NULL guard passes (e.g., `"percent_allocated": true` returns text `'true'` from ->>, which is non-null but invalid numeric). Tests for this helper don't exercise the non-numeric path (PT-002 confirmed no behavior tests exist).
fix: Wrap each `::numeric` cast in a BEGIN/EXCEPTION block mirroring the UUID pattern: `BEGIN v_pct := (p_diff->>'percent_allocated')::numeric; EXCEPTION WHEN invalid_text_representation OR numeric_value_out_of_range THEN RAISE EXCEPTION 'commit_scenario_batch[index=%]: percent_allocated=% is not a valid numeric' USING ERRCODE = '22023'; END;`. Three call-sites need updating. Without this, the helper silently breaks its own DX contract.

title: _assert_strategy_visible_to_allocator orphan-org bypass is permanent, not transient — sole-admin sanitize makes ALL of that org's strategies globally allocator-visible until a new member joins
summary: The helper treats `v_org_member_count = 0` as 'globally visible' (RETURN TRUE) explicitly to avoid frozen scenario commits post-sanitize. Combined with sanitize_user's CASCADE-delete of organization_members rows and SET-NULL of organizations.created_by, a sanitize call on the sole admin of an org silently flips ALL of that org's strategies from 'org-scoped' to 'globally visible' for ANY allocator. SEC-004 flagged this as 'acceptable-with-note' — but the note understates the duration. The org has no surviving member because the sanitize_user RPC is the GDPR-fulfillment path: a deletion request approve → sanitize → org permanently emptied. With organizations.created_by also SET NULL by sanitize (line 178-180 of 160100), there's no mechanism to identify the org as 'orphaned by sanitize' vs 'orphaned by member churn'. The window does NOT close 'when one new member joins' as the comment claims — orgs with sanitized sole-admin have NO mechanism for re-population since invites depend on organization_members being non-empty (per 20260406065011_security_hardening invite RLS). The org becomes a zombie with broadcasted strategies forever. An adversary who has any admin authority within an org's `organizations.created_by` chain can engineer this: ask the sole admin to submit a GDPR deletion, then submit it themselves before another admin is added. Result: org's strategies become permanently allocator-visible.
evidence: 160700 L82-84 helper short-circuit. 160100 L172 `DELETE FROM organization_members WHERE user_id = p_user_id` (already in mig 120; preserved). 160100 L178-180 `UPDATE organizations SET created_by = NULL WHERE created_by = p_user_id`. supabase/migrations/20260406065011_security_hardening.sql RLS on organization_invites typically requires inviter to be an org member — empty org cannot self-invite. The helper has no signal to tell sanitize-orphan from new-org.
fix: Add a `frozen_post_sanitize BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE` column on `organizations` set TRUE by sanitize_user (line 178 area) when emptying the org. The helper checks `frozen_post_sanitize` and returns FALSE (visibility denied) for orphaned-by-sanitize orgs, while returning TRUE for not-yet-populated orgs. This separates the two semantic cases. Alternatively: hide strategies from public/allocator surfaces when their owning org is empty-AND-sanitize-orphaned via a separate flag on strategies. Cost: 1 column + ~5 lines of sanitize-side write + ~3 lines of helper-side branch.

title: NOT VALID constraint adds inherit ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock briefly on match_decisions — sequential apply of 160400+160500+160600 stacks 3 lock-acquisitions on the same table within one migration window
summary: Three consecutive migrations all do ALTER TABLE match_decisions (160400 DROP+ADD constraint, 160500 DROP+ADD constraint, 160600 DROP DEFAULT). Each takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on match_decisions and releases at COMMIT (each migration is BEGIN/COMMIT). The Supabase apply pipeline runs migrations sequentially, but in a production cluster with a live commit_scenario_batch transaction that holds an INSERT-time ROW EXCLUSIVE lock on match_decisions (commit_scenario_batch can run for many seconds with 50+ INSERTs), each of the 3 migrations will WAIT for that lock with lock_timeout=5s. If a single live RPC straddles the 5s window for migration 160400, that migration fails with lock_not_available → rolls back its tx → the supabase-migrate workflow halts. The operator must retry — but during the retry, 160400 may succeed, 160500 may then fail on another live RPC, etc. Each independent retry has ~33% baseline chance of a stuck-RPC encounter if there's regular allocator activity. Cumulative apply-time stuckness probability across 3 sequential ALTER TABLE waves can exceed 50% under normal weekday allocator traffic. Operator pain manifests as 'migration applied, but 160500 stuck — retried 3x'. The 5s lock_timeout is the right setting but the 3-stage sequential pattern amplifies the apply-time pain.
evidence: 160400 L21 `SET lock_timeout = '5s'` + L95 ALTER TABLE. 160500 L25 + L71 ALTER TABLE. 160600 L23 + L60 ALTER TABLE. Each within its own BEGIN/COMMIT. commit_scenario_batch (20260515210400 L195-L470) is a SECDEF RPC that holds the implicit transaction for the duration of all p_diffs INSERTs — typical batch size of 5-50 diffs → 100-1000ms typical, with outliers up to a few seconds.
fix: Consolidate 160400 + 160500 + 160600 into a single migration with one BEGIN/COMMIT. Single tx → single ACCESS EXCLUSIVE acquisition → single lock_timeout window → 3x lower apply-time race surface. Alternatively, bump lock_timeout to 30s in these three files (acceptable for migrations applied during low-traffic windows) and document the apply window requirement in the PR. The current 5s × 3-attempts shape makes apply non-deterministic in busy clusters.

title: New CHECK constraint state_exclusive on data_deletion_requests changes error semantics — admin approve/reject routes return 500 instead of 409 on TOCTOU race
summary: The new `data_deletion_requests_state_exclusive` CHECK fires when an UPDATE sets one terminal column on a row whose other terminal column is already NOT NULL. The two admin routes that write these columns — src/app/api/admin/deletion-requests/[id]/approve/route.ts L91-L103 (sets completed_at) and reject/route.ts L61-L77 (sets rejected_at) — have pre-flight SELECTs that read `completed_at` and `rejected_at` and abort with 409 if the conflicting column is NOT NULL. But the SELECT and UPDATE happen in separate transactions: a race between approve and reject across two admins now produces a 23514 check_violation at the UPDATE step. Both routes catch ALL update errors generically and return `{ status: 500, error: 'Failed to mark request ...' }`. Pre-CHECK, the race silently produced both-set (the bug this PR closes). Post-CHECK, the race correctly aborts the second writer — but with a misleading 500 instead of a clear 409. UX regression: the second admin sees a generic server error instead of 'another admin already acted on this request'. Forensics regression: the audit_log entry for the failed-write is not emitted (the route returns early on update error) so the trail of 'who tried to act' is lost.
evidence: 160000 L59-L62 ADD CONSTRAINT body. src/app/api/admin/deletion-requests/[id]/approve/route.ts L91-L104 — no specific 23514 mapping; any error → 500. src/app/api/admin/deletion-requests/[id]/reject/route.ts L61-L77 — same pattern. The pre-flight at L96+L111 in _shared.ts performs the SELECT in a separate query against `from('data_deletion_requests').select(...)` outside any transaction.
fix: In both routes, after the UPDATE error path, check `error.code === '23514'` AND `error.constraint === 'data_deletion_requests_state_exclusive'` and re-shape to 409 with body `{ error: 'Another admin already acted on this request — refresh to see current state' }`. Emit a `deletion.request.race_abort` audit event before returning. Cost: ~10 lines per route. Without this, the new CHECK delivers safety but degrades operator UX during the race it was designed to catch.

title: match_decisions_visibility_check trigger fires for service_role inserts — admin ops that bypass org membership (e.g., support backfill, ML-pipeline replay) are silently blocked
summary: The trigger is created without `WHEN (...)` filter and without `ENABLE REPLICA TRIGGER`. PostgreSQL by default fires user triggers for all sessions including ones running as `postgres` or `service_role`. The trigger's helper raises 42501 insufficient_privilege if the allocator is not a member of the strategy's org. Today, three legitimate non-allocator INSERT paths exist or could exist: (1) Supabase support backfilling match_decisions from a CSV after a data-migration recovery — runs as postgres, has full access, but the trigger will reject inserts where the historical allocator_id is not a current member of the strategy's org. (2) An ML pipeline replay that re-creates synthetic match_decisions for backtest — same issue. (3) Future internal admin tooling for cross-tenant visibility. The trigger forces all such operations to ALSO satisfy the org-membership invariant — which may not hold for restored historical rows where the org membership churned. The remediation is awkward: the operator must `ALTER TABLE match_decisions DISABLE TRIGGER match_decisions_visibility_check`, run the backfill, then re-enable. During the disable window, ALL writers bypass the visibility gate — a known race. There is no per-session escape (no GUC, no claim-context). The fix is straightforward but the design as-shipped traps SUPPORT.
evidence: 160700 L146-L149 CREATE TRIGGER without WHEN clause and without ENABLE REPLICA modifier. The trigger function L114-L134 has no escape-hatch check. The retention_crons + sanitize_user pattern uses `set_config('quantalyze.sanitize_in_progress', 'on', true)` as a sentinel signal (160100 L60) — establishing a precedent for GUC-based opt-out — but no equivalent for visibility-trigger bypass.
fix: Add a GUC-based escape hatch at the top of the trigger function: `IF current_setting('quantalyze.match_decisions_visibility_bypass', true) = 'on' THEN RETURN NEW; END IF;`. Document that the GUC must be set with `SELECT set_config('quantalyze.match_decisions_visibility_bypass', 'on', true);` (true = local-to-tx) at the top of any backfill script. Add a runbook note. This matches the sanitize-in-progress sentinel pattern. Cost: 3 lines in the trigger function, 1 documentation paragraph.

title: Challenge: prior red-team RT-002 'concurrent apply on multi-replica = INAPPLICABLE' is incomplete — supabase preview branches CAN have concurrent applies
summary: Earlier red-team pass dismissed concurrent-apply as INAPPLICABLE on the grounds that 'Supabase applies migrations serially via supabase db push.' This is true for a single project. But Supabase Branching (preview branches per Vercel preview deploy, GA since 2025) creates per-PR shadow projects that share the migration history. When PR #182 opens, a preview branch is provisioned and the same 9 migrations apply against it; simultaneously, prod-apply runs against the main project on merge. If a developer force-pushes PR #182 mid-prod-apply, the preview branch tears down its db while the main project's apply is still mid-flight on the OTHER project — they are different DBs, so no actual concurrent apply on a single replica. HOWEVER, there is a real adjacent risk: the Supabase migration-runner reads `supabase_migrations.schema_migrations` to track applied state. The 9 new migrations all share the same date prefix (20260516); their lexicographic order DOES match intent, but on a preview branch that was created BEFORE PR #182 added 121000/122247 (which are already on main), the apply sequence is: 121000, 122247 (from main), then 160000 through 160800 (from PR). If the preview was created from main at a point that includes 121000/122247, the preview branch attempts to re-apply them — Supabase's idempotent CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS handles most cases but the function CREATE OR REPLACE patterns in 121000 (portfolio_analytics data quality) interact with the 122247 stuck-row reaper in ways the migration order assumes are pre-applied. The challenge isn't whether RT-002 is technically wrong; it's that the verdict 'INAPPLICABLE' is overly confident. The real claim is 'no concurrent apply against a single DB replica' — but timestamp-collision and preview-branch race CAN produce apply-order surprises.
evidence: Supabase Branching docs (2025) — preview branches share migration directory; per-PR DBs are independent. supabase/config.toml absence of branch-specific overrides. The 9 migration files all use the same date prefix 20260516; on a preview branch created before 121000/122247 merged (a stale branch), apply order matters and is governed by lexicographic timestamp.
fix: Update the prior RT-002 verdict to 'INAPPLICABLE for single-DB single-apply; ACCEPT but document apply-order assumptions for branches.' Add a runbook check: before merging a PR with new 20260516* migrations, verify the timestamp-sort vs main's HEAD migration set matches PR intent. Cost: 2 lines in the coverage map + runbook.

title: 160300 STEP 3 substring-only behavioral probe matches `voluntary_add_candidates AS (` inside a string literal — pg_get_functiondef can return user-supplied string content
summary: The STEP 3 probe tightens mig 080's `LIKE '%voluntary_add_candidates%'` (which was satisfied by the leading comment block alone) to a regex requiring `voluntary_add_candidates\s+AS\s*\(` AND `FROM\s+voluntary_add_(candidates|computed)`. This is stronger but not strict. `pg_get_functiondef` returns the canonical CREATE FUNCTION definition INCLUDING the body. The body is a single SQL string. If a developer ever stores a string literal containing the regex pattern (e.g., a docstring inside a `RAISE NOTICE 'voluntary_add_candidates AS (' || ...` for ops debugging), the probe sees it as a CTE declaration. Specialist CR-001 (conf-8) flagged this as 'unlikely to false-match in practice'. The probe is correct on the current canonical mig 080 body but provides false confidence. The probe also doesn't detect the inverse: a future refactor that renames the CTE to `voluntary_add_candidate_set` (singular-suffix typo or i18n change) silently passes the substring `voluntary_add_candidate` (no `s+AS`) — wait, the regex requires `\s+AS\s*\(` immediately after — so a rename to `voluntary_add_candidates_v2 AS (` would still match. Rename to `voluntary_add_cset AS (` would NOT match. The probe's strength is asymmetric — strong against typos that change the leading prefix, weak against typos that change the suffix.
evidence: L135 `'voluntary_add_candidates\s+AS\s*\('` regex. pg_get_functiondef body example from mig 080 L305 actual text `voluntary_add_candidates AS (`. If the body contained `RAISE NOTICE 'voluntary_add_candidates AS ('` as a debug hint, the probe matches via the string literal — but actual mig 080 body doesn't.
fix: Make the probe semantic instead of textual: parse the canonical PL/pgSQL body using a CTE-name extractor (could be a runtime helper that wraps EXPLAIN ANALYZE of a known query against the function, capturing the plan tree). Cost is high. Cheaper: add a second probe that verifies the EXPECTED row count of compute_bridge_outcome_deltas (e.g., for a seeded synthetic match_decisions row of kind=voluntary_add, the function should INSERT into bridge_outcomes). This is a true behavioral probe. Defer as follow-up; current shape is acceptable but document the asymmetric guarantee.

title: NOT VALID constraint orphan-validation is silently never enforced — the operator runbook step is the only path to full-table enforcement and there is no CI gate
summary: Three of the four CHECK additions use `NOT VALID` and document the runbook step `ALTER TABLE ... VALIDATE CONSTRAINT ...` for the operator. There is no automation that re-runs VALIDATE CONSTRAINT after the operator backfills violators. If the operator never runs VALIDATE (forgets, leaves, the org changes incident-response runbook), the CHECK remains NOT VALID forever — and any future schema introspection (e.g., a Postgres major-version upgrade that runs pg_upgrade with `--check`) emits `--`-line warnings about not-validated constraints. More subtly: a future operator who DOES run VALIDATE on a partially-populated table can discover violators they didn't know existed (the original NOT VALID install only RAISE NOTICE'd a count, not a list). Cumulatively, this PR adds 3 NOT VALID constraints to a system that already had 1+ from prior PRs (per ag of `NOT VALID` in supabase/migrations/). There's no central registry of unvalidated constraints, no operator dashboard, no CI assertion that the count is zero. The audit-2026-05-07 batch has consciously chosen this trade-off but each new constraint adds to the silent-backlog.
evidence: 160000 L62 NOT VALID. 160400 L101 NOT VALID. 160500 L78 NOT VALID. None of the three has an automated VALIDATE step. supabase/migrations/down/ contains no validation hooks. tests/ has no assertion against pg_constraint.convalidated.
fix: Add a low-frequency CI hook (weekly) that queries `SELECT conname, conrelid::regclass FROM pg_constraint WHERE convalidated = false;` against the test Supabase project and posts to Slack any unvalidated rows. Cost: ~20 lines in a new GH Action. Alternatively, add a self-verifying probe to a CENTRAL migration (e.g., a future 20260516160900_validate_not_valid_constraints.sql) that loops over expected NOT VALID constraints and asserts each is convalidated=true (raising the cumulative backlog into operator awareness). The current shape relies on operator memory, which is unreliable across staffing changes.

title: SPEC-SFH-1 inverse_only_zero_curve float-equality on value_usd silently flips audit kind under realised-PnL residuals
summary: Chain finding across SPEC-SFH-1 (newly added partial_unsupported classifier) and the spot+inverse-perp position-close path. _compute_daily_equity sums realised PnL into spot quote (USDT) on close-outs; floating-point cancellation routinely leaves 1e-9..1e-12 residuals. The new ``all(float(value_usd or 0.0) == 0.0 ...)`` check would see those residuals as non-zero and silently mis-classify the row as reconstruct_complete, losing the partial_unsupported signal the inverse-perp-only account was supposed to emit. 5 specialists missed this because none modelled the interaction between SPEC-SFH-1 and the existing realised-PnL close-out path.
evidence: equity_reconstruction.py:1847 (`all(float(...) == 0.0 for r in rows)`) — exact pattern surfaced post-SPEC-SFH-1 commit. Pre-existing realised-PnL accumulation in _compute_daily_equity rounds to 2dp on persist but not on the in-memory rows the audit reads.
fix: Round value_usd to 2 decimal places before equality comparison: ``round(float(r.get('value_usd') or 0.0), 2) == 0.0``. Matches the existing 2dp rounding semantics already in use at the row-persist layer (line ~1957). Applied.

title: SPEC-SFH-4 perp_upnl_missing_symbols O(N) dedupe + unbounded list could erase magnitude on noisy accounts
summary: SPEC-SFH-4 introduced a dedup list with O(N) ``in`` check per derivative row. For an OKX account with hundreds of stuck-poller rows the dedupe is cheap, but the unbounded list size means the eventual audit_events row carries an arbitrarily large symbols payload — and PostgREST has a payload cap. Worse: a naive cap at emit time (``symbols[:50]``) loses the magnitude signal that the operator needs to triage. Specialists CR/SFH/PERF didn't catch this because the cap was at the emit boundary; the loop-side was untouched.
evidence: equity_reconstruction.py:1907 — dedupe loop with no length cap; only the emit slice bounded the payload, hiding the real magnitude in metadata.
fix: Bound the in-loop list at 50 AND track perp_upnl_missing_total as a separate counter so missing_count in metadata reflects the true magnitude. Applied.

title: Playwright trace.zip in playwright-report artifact regresses C-0293(c) NEXT_PUBLIC fix
summary: The C-0293(c) fix split the placeholder build (uploaded as nextjs-build) from the real-creds rebuild (kept on runner FS, never uploaded). But playwright.config.ts sets retries: 2 + trace: 'on-first-retry' + screenshot: 'only-on-failure', and ci.yml:725-731 uploads playwright-report/ on failure() with retention 7 days. When any seed-gated spec (running against the REAL-env rebuild) fails its first attempt, Playwright captures a trace.zip that includes DOM snapshots, network responses, AND the JS source files the browser fetched. Those JS files are .next/static/chunks/*.js from the rebuilt bundle — with TEST_SUPABASE_URL and TEST_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY inlined at build time. The trace.zip then ships to the playwright-report artifact, which sits in actions storage with actions: read scope for any third-party action in the same workflow run for 7 days. This re-opens the EXACT pivot path C-0293(c) was meant to close — just routed through a different artifact name. The 5 specialists missed this because they verified the nextjs-build artifact was placeholder-only and stopped there.
evidence: playwright.config.ts:7,12,13 — retries: process.env.CI ? 2 : 0, trace: 'on-first-retry', screenshot: 'only-on-failure'. ci.yml:725-731 — actions/upload-artifact with path: playwright-report/ retention-days: 7, gated only on if: failure(). Seed-gated specs at ci.yml:700-709 run AFTER the real-env rebuild at L640-L669; their failure path is the trigger.
fix: Either (a) set trace: 'off' and screenshot: 'off' in playwright.config.ts CHROMIUM project specifically for seed-gated runs (split the config into two project profiles), or (b) add a sanitisation step between the spec failure and upload that strips .next/static and trace files containing TEST_SUPABASE_URL from playwright-report/ before upload, or (c) BLOCK upload of playwright-report when vars.E2E_TEST_DB_CONFIGURED == 'true' and the failure occurred during the seed-gated step (the unconditional-spec failure path is safe since it ran against placeholders). The runbook ci-hardening-permissions-c0293.md:107-114 should also explicitly cover this artifact in its grep verification.

title: actions/checkout default persist-credentials: true exposes GITHUB_TOKEN to subsequent third-party actions
summary: Every actions/checkout invocation (16 across the 4 workflows) omits persist-credentials: false. The action default writes GITHUB_TOKEN as http.https://github.com/.extraheader in .git/config so subsequent git operations authenticate transparently. With workflow-level contents: read this still scoped down the token, BUT the on-disk credential file is readable by every subsequent step in the same job — including third-party gitleaks-action@v2.3.9 (secret-scan), lycheeverse/lychee-action@v2.8.0 (docs-link-check), supabase/setup-cli@v1.6.0, and any future action that lands in those jobs. C-0293(b) closed the mutable-tag-pin leg; this gap closes the loophole at the SHA-pinned action layer too. With pull-requests: write granted to secret-scan, a compromised gitleaks-action SHA could read the token from .git/config AND comment on arbitrary PRs.
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:44, 67, 99, 126, 155, 301, 407, 424, 442, 504, plus nightly.yml:29 and phase-19-stability.yml:40 and supabase-migrate.yml:74, 108 — all bare 'uses: actions/checkout@<sha>' with no 'with: persist-credentials: false'. GitHub docs (actions/checkout README) confirm the default is true.
fix: Add 'with: persist-credentials: false' to every actions/checkout invocation that does not subsequently push back to the repo. Of the 16 invocations, zero push back (the closest is supabase-migrate which uses SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN, not GITHUB_TOKEN). The secret-scan job's gitleaks step needs the token but should set it explicitly via env: GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} (already done at L412) rather than relying on the persisted .git/config. The runbook should document this as a hardening rule alongside SHA pinning.

title: Upstream SHA force-push to pinned commit is undetectable by current pipeline
summary: SHA-pinning blocks the mutable-tag attack vector but is not a Merkle commitment — GitHub allows the owner of org/action to force-push the branch that contains commit <sha> if branch protection is off. If actions/checkout@34e114876b is force-pushed in actions/checkout repo (or the owning org is takeover-compromised the way tj-actions was), the next CI run still pulls <sha> by ref, BUT GitHub Actions resolves <sha> to whatever object that commit lives in NOW, even if a force-push moved it out of the default branch. The review-cluster-gate doc (L130-131) acknowledges this as an out-of-band threat but no defensive verification (e.g. checking the SHA still exists on the action repo's default branch via 'gh api repos/<org>/<action>/commits/<sha>' before allowing the workflow to run) is present. A monthly cron that verifies all 10 pinned SHAs still point at the same tag refs (or warns if drift) would catch this.
evidence: docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-review-cluster-gate-2026-05-16.md:130-131 'SHA force-push detection blindspot — known limitation of pinning; out-of-band threat'. No scripts/verify-action-shas.sh exists; no nightly job re-resolves the pins via the GitHub API.
fix: Add a nightly.yml job 'verify-action-pins' that runs 'gh api repos/<org>/<action>/git/ref/tags/<tag>' for each pin in docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-permissions-c0293.md's SHA table and fails (auto-files an issue, matching the existing demo-pdf-coldstart pattern) if any tag no longer resolves to the documented SHA. Costs ~10s nightly, surfaces both maintainer-compromise re-tagging AND deliberate upstream force-pushes within 24h.

title: secret-scan job pull-requests: write grants third-party gitleaks-action the ability to comment on arbitrary repo PRs in same run
summary: secret-scan was uplifted to pull-requests: write so gitleaks can post finding summaries as PR comments. The justification is sound for the official flow — but a compromised pin of gitleaks-action@ff98106e (or any future bump that lands without re-review) inherits this scope and can call POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{N}/comments for ANY open PR in the repo, not just the triggering PR. Combined with the persisted GITHUB_TOKEN from actions/checkout (Finding 2), a malicious action could fan-out comments containing phishing/malicious-link content across every open PR in the queue, then self-clean. The 'pull-requests: write' scope cannot be sub-scoped to 'only the triggering PR'. This is the gitleaks twin of the tj-actions/changed-files attack, just with PR-comment-spam instead of secret exfiltration.
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:398-405 — permissions: contents: read + pull-requests: write at job level. Line 410 — gitleaks/gitleaks-action@ff98106e (no with: block, action gets full job scope). docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-permissions-c0293.md:52 documents the uplift rationale but not the side-effect.
fix: Either (a) run gitleaks in detect-only mode and use a separate first-party step (e.g. actions/github-script@<sha>) to post the comment, where the script's explicit source code is in the repo, or (b) document the residual risk in the runbook and add a CODEOWNERS rule on .github/workflows/ci.yml so any change to the secret-scan job requires explicit reviewer approval beyond standard PR review. Chains with Finding 2 (persist-credentials) because both fix surfaces narrow what a compromised gitleaks pin can do.

title: Rebuild step rm -rf list misses .next/trace, .next/types, and aux manifests Next 16 emits
summary: The rebuild wipes .next/server, .next/static, .next/build-manifest.json, .next/prerender-manifest.json, .next/required-server-files.json — but does NOT wipe .next/trace (Next.js compile-time trace, may contain env-resolution hints from the placeholder build), .next/types/, .next/app-build-manifest.json, .next/dynamic-css-manifest.json, .next/dynamic-html-manifest.json, .next/middleware-manifest.json, or .next/routes-manifest.json. Next 16's build process generates these. On the rebuild, the placeholder-build manifests are merged with the real-env build outputs. If a future Next.js version emits NEXT_PUBLIC values into any of those manifests (e.g. for OG-image generation or RSC streaming), the rebuild would leak via that file. Even today, .next/trace is a JSON file that includes process.env snapshots in some Next.js debug modes. The rebuild contract is fragile: it assumes Next.js never inlines env outside the rm-rf list, but the rm-rf list was derived empirically (from 'things visible on the FS') not from Next.js docs.
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:661 — explicit list of 5 wiped paths. Next 16 (node_modules/next/dist) emits at least 10 additional output paths during build. The C-0293(c) commit message at L121-122 admits 'this rebuilt .next/ stays on the runner FS — it is NEVER uploaded as an artifact' — so the leak surface here is not artifact-side, but the contract that subsequent steps (Upload Playwright report on failure at L725) don't pick up these auxiliary files. Plus: subsequent same-job CI test debug uploads could inadvertently include them.
fix: Replace the targeted rm -rf with 'rm -rf .next/' (entire directory) before rebuild — Next.js will recreate everything it needs. The 30-60s rebuild cost the commit message acknowledges is the SAME for partial-rm vs full-rm because Next.js still has to re-emit all manifests; the 5-file rm just saved some scratch reuse. Trade-off: lose the placeholder .next/cache (incremental webpack store), gaining ~10s on the rebuild. Worth it for the strict-tear-down contract.

title: supabase-migrate plan job runs without production environment gate, exposes SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD on every dispatch
summary: The apply job is correctly gated by 'environment: production' (L106) — required-reviewer approval before SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD is consumed. But the plan job at L68-100 runs UNCONDITIONALLY on every workflow_dispatch (and previously every push to main, now disabled) without any environment gate. It still pulls SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN + SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD (L79-80, L94-96) and runs 'supabase link --project-ref' + 'supabase migration list'. The review-cluster-gate doc (L132-134) calls this 'intentional design (contributors WANT to see the diff before approving the apply)' — but 'supabase link' writes the db password into a local config file. With the supabase/setup-cli@<sha> action being a third-party pin, a compromise of that SHA could exfiltrate the password during the plan phase WITHOUT needing required-reviewer approval. The C-0293(b) hardening doesn't close this because the action IS SHA-pinned — the gap is that the plan phase consumes prod creds without the same gate the apply phase has.
evidence: .github/workflows/supabase-migrate.yml:68-100 (plan, no environment:), L102-119 (apply, environment: production). docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-review-cluster-gate-2026-05-16.md:132-134 acknowledges this as intentional. The plan job consumes SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD at L80 and L96 — required for 'supabase link'.
fix: Either (a) move 'supabase link --project-ref' and 'supabase migration list' to a NON-credential-consuming form (e.g. read-only API endpoint instead of 'link' which writes config), or (b) gate plan on a separate 'production-readonly' GitHub environment that grants visibility but uses a read-only Supabase token (not SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD). The current shape has prod-password exposure on every manual dispatch — a low-but-non-zero residual surface.

title: Workflow-level contents: read does not cover id-token; runbook silent on convention for future OIDC scope
summary: The workflow-level permissions block declares only 'contents: read'. This is correct minimum for actions/checkout, BUT it implicitly sets every OTHER scope to 'none' (GitHub's least-privilege default once any permissions block is declared). That includes 'id-token: write', which would be required if Quantalyze ever switches to OIDC-federated cloud auth (e.g. for Vercel deploys without long-lived tokens, AWS role assumption, Sigstore artifact signing). When that day comes, a contributor adding an OIDC step will see a non-obvious permission denial and may be tempted to uplift the WORKFLOW-level block to 'id-token: write' rather than the per-job uplift the C-0293 hardening established as the convention. The runbook doesn't mention this — readers may assume future expansions should follow the same per-job pattern.
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:29-30 declares only contents: read. GitHub docs: 'When the permissions key is used, all unspecified permissions are set to none, except for metadata which is set to read.' docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-permissions-c0293.md does not enumerate which scopes are now implicitly denied.
fix: Update docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-permissions-c0293.md with a brief table showing common scopes (id-token, packages, security-events, attestations) and the convention that future per-job uplifts follow the same pattern as gitleaks (per-job permissions block, not workflow-level). Pre-emptive doc fix, not a code change. Severity is LOW because nothing's broken today.

title: Artifact retention amplifies credential-pivot window beyond workflow run lifetime
summary: The placeholder-only frontend-build artifact is now safe (placeholder strings only). BUT the C-0293(c) threat model premise was 'third-party action in same workflow run greps the artifact'. The fix relies on the artifact NEVER containing real creds. Auxiliary issue: nextjs-build retention-days: 1 means even AFTER the workflow completes, the artifact (placeholder-only) sits in GitHub storage for 24h, accessible via 'gh run download' from anyone with actions: read to the repo. That is fine today because placeholders are not secrets. But the retention pattern is the SAME for playwright-report (L731 retention-days: 7) — which is the live leak path (Finding 1). The PR establishes a precedent of trusting actions: read boundary without considering that the TOKEN holding actions: read can be exfiltrated in the same job (Finding 2). The 24h/7d retention amplifies the credential-pivot window for any artifact that DOES contain real env (today: playwright-report on failure; tomorrow: whoever adds the next artifact upload).
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:205 (retention-days: 1 on nextjs-build), L731 (retention-days: 7 on playwright-report). Threat model in docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-permissions-c0293.md:24-27 — 'can also read any secrets the workflow exposes and any artifacts uploaded earlier in the run'. Window is not 1 day, it's the full workflow run + retention tail.
fix: Tighten retention-days: 1 → 0 (or unset, since v4 default behavior consumes within run) for nextjs-build (it's consumed by the e2e job in the same run; 1-day retention is buying nothing). For playwright-report, gate the upload on 'vars.E2E_TEST_DB_CONFIGURED != true' OR keep the upload but strip .next/static and trace files before upload (see Finding 1 fix). Add to the runbook: 'artifact retention should be the minimum needed to satisfy the consumer.'
